title: C extension swallows exceptions from `PyObject_IsInstance` in `FunctionWrapper.__init__` and bound-method dispatch
author: GrahamDumpleton
body:
## Summary

Two call sites in `src/wrapt/_wrappers.c` invoke `PyObject_IsInstance` without checking for the `-1` error return:

1. **`WraptFunctionWrapper_init`**  — the cascade that picks a `binding` ("function", "classmethod", "class", "staticmethod", "instancemethod", ...) uses bare `else if (PyObject_IsInstance(wrapped, ...))`. Because `-1` is truthy in C, an exception raised from a metaclass's `__instancecheck__` is silently treated as "yes, this is an instance". The wrong binding is recorded *and* a Python exception is left set on the thread state, which then surfaces later as a spurious failure in unrelated code.

2. **`WraptBoundFunctionWrapper_call`** — uses `if (PyObject_IsInstance(instance, self->owner) == 1)`. The `== 1` avoids the truthy trap, but the `-1` error case is silently swallowed: the partial-binding branch is skipped, the stale exception is left set, and execution continues into the regular call path.

Both sites should capture the result, propagate `-1` / `NULL` on error, and only take the "true" branch on a positive result.

## Credits

Thanks to @devdanzin for the report via:

* https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/e1ac0c43dc4d4278ee82455da4859872
